news_summary: Innovative circular economy startups, breakthroughs in green hydrogen technology, the discovery of climate-beneficial plants, and the preservation of ancient species highlight promising advances in sustainability and environmental conservation.
news_summary_fr: Les start-ups innovantes dans le domaine de l'économie circulaire, les avancées révolutionnaires dans la technologie de l'hydrogène vert, la découverte de plantes bénéfiques pour le climat et la préservation d'espèces anciennes mettent en évidence des progrès prometteurs en matière de durabilité et de préservation de l'environnement.
news_summary_es: Las innovadoras empresas emergentes dedicadas a la economía circular, los avances en la tecnología del hidrógeno verde, el descubrimiento de plantas beneficiosas para el clima y la preservación de especies antiguas ponen de relieve los prometedores avances en materia de sostenibilidad y conservación del medio ambiente.

articles.title: Circular Economy Startups to Watch in 2026
articles.summary:
The circular economy grew 7.5% in the past year and now employs more than 2.2...
The post Circular Economy Startups to Watch in 2026 appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
The circular economy grew 7.5% in the past year and now employs more than 2.2 million people globally, according to StartUs Insights’ 2025 Circular Economy Report. With the North American market projected to grow by 25.65% through 2031, investors are placing increasingly bold bets on startups that aim to close material loops across fashion, electronics, packaging, energy, and waste management.

Here are the circular economy startups that earned the most recognition in 2025 and, if you dig on innovation, deserve your attention in 2026.

Circ

Circ has created a patented hydrothermal process that separates and recovers polyester and cotton from blended polycotton textiles used in most modern garments. The process produces raw materials that are as useful as new textiles in clothing. Circ is currently the only textile-to-textile recycler that can handle polycotton blends, recovering both materials. In May 2025, the company announced plans to build the world’s first industrial-scale polycotton recycling facility in Saint-Avold, France, with the capacity to process 70,000 metric tons of material each year.

Reported impact

Circ works with Zara, H&M Group, Mara Hoffman, and Christian Siriano to make recycled fibers more common in fashion. The company’s pilot program led by UNIDO recycled 2.6 tons of polycotton manufacturing waste from H&M’s suppliers in Bangladesh. Since polycotton blends make up 77% of the global textile market, but less than 1% of all textiles are recycled back into textiles, Circ’s scalable solution could make a big impact, making fast fashion less damaging.

Recognition

Circ was a 2023 Earthshot Prize Finalist and was named a Circ was a 2023 Earthshot Prize Finalist and was named a 2024 Fast Company Most Innovative Company and BloombergNEF Pioneer. The company was also recognized by TIME and Statista as a Top GreenTech Company and is a Certified B Corporation. In March 2025, Circ closed a $25 million funding round and the company’s first commercial-scale plant was announced by France’s Macron administration at the 2025 Choose France Summit.

SOLARCYCLE

SOLARCYCLE is solving the photovoltaic panel recycles problem with a process that recovers up to 95% of valuable materials in solar panels, including aluminum, glass, copper, silver, and silicon. The metals and silicon are put back into the U.S. solar supply chain, reducing the nation’s dependence of imported materials. The company runs a processing facility in Odessa, Texas, and plans to open a larger facility in Georgia that can process up to 10 million panels each year. SOLARCYCLE is also building the first crystalline silicon solar glass factory in the U.S., which will use recycled glass to produce up to 5 gigawatts of new solar glass.

Reported impact

In 2024, SOLARCYCLE recycled over 480,000 solar panels and prevented 13.8 million kg of CO₂e emissions. The company expected to recycle nearly one million panels by the end of 2025 and twice that number in 2026. More than 90 energy companies have chosen SOLARCYCLE as their recycling partner. By 2050, decommissioned solar panels could reach 80 million metric tons worldwide, creating a huge opportunity for material recovery.

Recognition

OLARCYCLE was named to the 2025 Global Cleantech 100 by Cleantech Group and was the only solar panel recycling company on TIME’s list of America’s Top GreenTech Companies 2025. The company was also featured in the StartUs Insights 2025 Circular Economy Report as one of five innovative startups. SOLARCYCLE has raised over $80 million in equity funding from investors including Microsoft, along with a $1.5 million DOE grant.

Greyparrot

Greyparrot uses AI-powered camera systems on conveyor belts in recycling facilities to identify and track more than 111 waste materials in real time. Its Analyzer platform helps facilities sort more accurately and recover more materials, while the Sync system adds AI intelligence to existing sorting machines. In 2025, Greyparrot launched Deepnest, the first AI-powered packaging waste intelligence platform for consumer brands, which helps companies like Unilever, L’Oréal, and Asahi Group track how their packaging performs in real-world recycling systems.

Reported impact

Greyparrot’s systems have identified more than 100 billion waste objects so far. The company has 187 Analyzers operating in over 20 countries that have detected about $1 billion in recyclable materials in the waste streams the monitors. The company estimates that using its systems worldwide could reveal up to $100 billion in recyclable value each year by 2040.

Recognition

Greyparrot was named one of TIME’s Best Inventions 2025 and was selected for Fast Company’s World’s Most Innovative Companies 2025. The company ranked #104 on the Financial Times list of fastest-growing European startups in 2025. Greyparrot has raised a total of $27.2 million in funding to date.

PulpaTronics

PulpaTronics makes RFID tags made entirely from paper. Traditional RFID tags use mined metals and silicon chips, which create electronic waste and can contaminate recycling when attached to packaging. PulpaTronics’ tags use laser-cut paper for product tracking, so they can be fully recycled when mixed with regular paper waste.

Reported impact

Paper tags remove the need for metal mining in RFID production, make manufacturing simpler, and eliminate a major source of e-waste in packaging. As retail and logistics companies use more RFID for inventory management, PulpaTronics can provide a scalable way to keep these systems compatible with circular material flows.

Recognition

PulpaTronics won the 2024 Green Alley Award, Europe’s longest-running circular economy startup prize, chosen from a record 339 applicants. The company was also named a startup winner at the Santander X Global Challenge Circular Economy Revolution in June 2025, sharing €120,000 in prizes with five other companies.

traceless materials

A Hamburg, Germany-based startup, traceless materials makes a natural biomaterial plastic alternative using agricultural industry leftovers, such as grain processing byproducts. The traceless® material is certified 100% bio-based, home-compostable, and plastic-free. After use, the material can be processed with standard plastics industry equipment to make rigid molded parts, flexible films, paper coatings, and adhesives.

Reported impact

Traceless® reduces CO₂ emissions by 91% and fossil energy use during production by 89%. The material contains no harmful chemicals and does not compete for grain with food production, using the leftovers instead. With 75 employees and its first large-scale plant being built, traceless is now moving from pilot to commercial production and aims to replace thousands of tons of conventional plastic a year. The company has announced a partnership with global packaging manufacturer Mondi.

Recognition

Traceless won the 2021 Green Alley Award and received a €2.42 million EIC Accelerator grant from the European Innovation Council. The company also won the German Entrepreneur Award and the German Sustainability Award/Next Economy Award. In 2023, Traceless secured €36.6 million in Series A funding, plus about €5 million from the German Federal Ministry for the Environment’s Innovation Programme.

Kelpi

Kelpi, based in Bristol, UK, has developed recyclable barrier coatings—the stuff that makes packaging moisture resistant and shiny—made from seaweed. These coatings replace fossil-fuel-based plastics on paper and cardboard packaging. Kelpi’s biomaterials resist water, oxygen, grease, and acid, matching or even exceeding the performance of regular plastic coatings, while remaining fully recyclable and home-compostable. Because seaweed grows without requiring fertilizers, fresh water, or farmland, and absorbs a lot of CO₂ as it grows, the raw material Kelpi is made from is environmentally friendly.

Reported impact

Kelpi has research and development contracts with L’Oréal, Diageo, and Waitrose, and is moving its manufacturing pilots toward commercial launch in the next year. The technology solves a key problem in paper packaging recycling, because plastic barrier coatings are not recyclable. Kelpi only uses seaweed from sustainable farms, which also helps reduce ocean acidification.

Recognition

Kelpi was named a scaleup winner at the Santander X Global Challenge | Circular Economy Revolution in June 2025. The company has raised about £9.5 million ($12.7 million), including funding from Innovate UK.

Protein Evolution

Protein Evolution (PEI) uses AI-designed enzymes to break down plastic and polyester textile waste into their original chemical building blocks, called monomers, which can be reused to make new plastics. The company focuses on polyester waste, such as PET bottles, clamshell containers, and synthetic textiles. The company’s has AI mapped tens of millions of unique enzymes to find the best ways to handle different types of waste, providing a biological alternative to energy-intensive mechanical and chemical recycling methods.

Reported impact

PEI says its enzyme-based recycling process uses little energy and produces no emissions, representing a different way to tackle the plastics problem than approaches that rely on heat, which generates CO2 emissions. If this technology works at scale, it could help recycle the roughly 87% of clothing that now ends up in incinerators or landfills.

Recognition

Protein Evolution was featured in the StartUs Insights 2025 Circular Economy Report as one of five highlighted startups. The company has $20 million in seed funding.

Radical Dot

Radical Dot, another German startup, converts mixed plastic waste, including hard-to-recycle PVC and polystyrene that usually go to landfill or incineration, into chemicals that can be used to make new plastics, replacing fossil-based materials in industrial chemical production. The approach reduces the need for new petrochemicals and can help prevent plastic waste, particular polystyrene foam and peanuts, from reaching the environment.

Reported impact

By focusing on mixed plastic waste that current mechanical recycling systems cannot process, Radical Dot fills a gap in today’s recycling infrastructure. The technology aims to cut both fossil fuel use and harmful emissions linked to making new chemicals from oil.

Recognition

Radical Dot was named a startup winner at the Santander X Global Challenge | Circular Economy Revolution in June 2025, along with PulpaTronics and Metalchemy, sharing €120,000 in prizes.

Glacier

Featured on Sustainability In Your Ear, December 2025

Co-founded by Rebecca Hu-Thrams and Areeb Malik, Glacier has developed AI-powered robotic sorters for material recovery facilities (MRFs). The robots use computer vision trained on more than 3 billion images of real recycling waste to identify and sort over 70 types of materials at up to 45 picks per minute, around the clock. These robots work in environments where injury rates for human workers are twice as high as in construction. Glacier’s systems also generate brand- and package-level data about what is actually in the waste stream to help brands like Amazon see which packaging designs are truly recyclable in practice.

Reported impact

Glacier’s robots now process recycling for about one in ten American households. At a Detroit MRF, AI cameras on a residue line revealed large PET losses the facility suspected but had never measured. Adding just one robotic sorter cut PET going to landfill by two-thirds and brought in $138,000 in extra annual revenue. Most facilities see a return on investment in less than a year. As Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) laws expand in the U.S., with seven states now requiring producers to fund and document packaging recycling, Glacier’s brand-level waste data will become essential for compliance.

Recognition

Glacier was named one of TIME’s Best Inventions 2025. The company raised $16 million in Series A funding and $7.7 million in earlier funding from NEA and Amazon’s Climate Pledge Fund. Glacier has also been featured in TechCrunch, Fast Company, and Waste360.

CurbWaste

Featured on Sustainability In Your Ear, February 2026

CurbWaste, founded by fourth-generation waste industry professional Mike Marmo, is building an operating system for independent waste haulers. These family-run businesses are the backbone of America’s waste collection system. The company’s CurbPOS platform combines order management, real-time dispatch, automated billing, and materials tracking in one place, and generates automated LEED diversion reports and Recycling Certification Institute documentation. The data it delivers provides the per-load, per-material chain-of-custody data that EPR programs need.

Reported impact

CurbWaste serves over 150 haulers in 40 states, bringing digital tools to a trillion-dollar industry that has seen little technology adoption. By standardizing data flows for different municipal and environmental reporting needs, the platform creates the transparency the circular economy needs to work. It connects collection systems to processing facilities and establishes the material traceability needed for EPR compliance across the country. Marmo sees CurbWaste becoming the waste industry’s main system of record, similar to how Salesforce is used in CRM.

Recognition

CurbWaste raised a $28 million Series B round in October 2025, brings the company’s total funding to $50 million. CurbWaste has been featured in Axios, Waste Dive, PYMNTS, and Resource Recycling.

Today’s Innovators Lead a Growing Movement

This year’s most-recognized circular economy startups stand out for the wide range of material challenges they address, from polycotton textiles and solar panels to paper packaging coatings and mixed plastic waste. The companies that have attracted the most capital and recognition have something in common, they have moved beyond proof-of-concept and shown commercial viability by signing agreements with major brands and building their first industrial-scale facilities.

The circular economy is still a small part of the global materials system, only about 7.2% of used materials are currently cycled back into the economy, according to the Circle Economy Foundation. However, with new policies like EPR regulations gaining ground in Europe and a growing number of U.S. states, and over $670 million in venture funding going to circular economy startups in 2025 alone, the companies on this list are well positioned to show that circularity can work at scale; and then watch the floodgates open to bring a host of new circular choices to you.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights significant advancements by multiple startups in the circular economy, showcasing scalable innovations in recycling and sustainable materials that have broad environmental benefits. It details concrete impacts, partnerships with major brands, funding milestones, and industrial-scale projects, indicating meaningful progress toward reducing waste and emissions globally. The scope covers various materials and industries, emphasizing systemic change and policy alignment, making it an inspiring and substantial environmental good news story.
articles.personality_title: Startups Driving Big Changes in Recycling and Sustainable Materials for 2026
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The circular economy is a system where waste materials are reused or recycled to make new products, helping to reduce pollution and save resources. Recently, this area has been growing quickly, with more people working in it and more money invested in new ideas.

**What happened** – In 2025, several startups made important progress in recycling and sustainable materials. For example, Circ created a way to recycle blended fabrics like polycotton, which are usually hard to recycle. SOLARCYCLE recycles solar panels to recover valuable metals. Greyparrot uses artificial intelligence (AI) to sort waste better in recycling centers. Other startups make paper-based tags to replace electronic ones, seaweed-based coatings for packaging, and enzymes that break down plastic waste.

**Impact** – These startups are helping reduce waste and pollution in different industries like fashion, electronics, and packaging. They have signed deals with big companies like Zara, H&M, and L’Oréal, and are building factories that can handle large amounts of materials. For example, Circ’s new plant in France will recycle 70,000 tons of textiles a year, and SOLARCYCLE plans a facility to recycle millions of solar panels. Their work means fewer materials end up in landfills or polluting the environment.

**What's next step** – Many of these companies are moving from testing their ideas to producing at a large scale. New laws in Europe and the U.S. will also encourage more recycling and make companies responsible for their packaging waste. With over $670 million invested in circular economy startups in 2025, these innovations could soon become common, changing how we make and reuse products.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Innovative startups are turning waste into valuable resources on a large scale, helping industries recycle more and reduce environmental harm in the coming years.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des startups qui transforment le recyclage et les matériaux durables en 2026
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'économie circulaire est un système qui permet de réutiliser ou recycler les matériaux usagés pour fabriquer de nouveaux produits, réduisant ainsi la pollution et économisant les ressources. Ce secteur connaît une forte croissance avec de plus en plus d’acteurs et d’investissements.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En 2025, plusieurs startups ont fait des progrès importants dans le recyclage et les matériaux durables. Par exemple, Circ a inventé un procédé pour recycler les tissus mélangés comme le polycoton, habituellement difficiles à recycler. SOLARCYCLE recycle les panneaux solaires pour récupérer des métaux précieux. Greyparrot utilise l’intelligence artificielle pour mieux trier les déchets dans les centres de recyclage. D’autres startups produisent des étiquettes en papier, des revêtements à base d’algues pour les emballages, ou des enzymes qui décomposent les plastiques.

**Impact** – Ces startups aident à réduire les déchets et la pollution dans plusieurs secteurs comme la mode, l’électronique et l’emballage. Elles travaillent avec de grandes marques comme Zara, H&M et L’Oréal, et construisent des usines capables de traiter de grandes quantités de matériaux. Par exemple, l’usine de Circ en France pourra recycler 70 000 tonnes de textiles par an, et SOLARCYCLE prévoit une installation pour recycler des millions de panneaux solaires. Leur travail évite que ces matériaux finissent en décharge ou polluent l’environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces entreprises passent de la phase de test à la production à grande échelle. De nouvelles lois en Europe et aux États-Unis encouragent aussi davantage le recyclage et responsabilisent les producteurs d’emballages. Avec plus de 670 millions de dollars investis en 2025 dans ces startups, ces innovations pourraient bientôt se généraliser et changer notre manière de fabriquer et de réutiliser les produits.

**Une phrase clé** – Des startups innovantes transforment les déchets en ressources précieuses à grande échelle, aidant les industries à recycler plus et à réduire leur impact environnemental dans les prochaines années.
articles.personality_title_es: Startups que impulsan grandes cambios en reciclaje y materiales sostenibles para 2026
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La economía circular es un sistema donde los materiales de desecho se reutilizan o reciclan para crear nuevos productos, ayudando a reducir la contaminación y ahorrar recursos. Este sector ha crecido rápidamente con más personas trabajando y más dinero invertido en nuevas ideas.

**Qué pasó** – En 2025, varias startups lograron avances importantes en reciclaje y materiales sostenibles. Por ejemplo, Circ desarrolló un método para reciclar telas mezcladas como el policotón, que normalmente son difíciles de reciclar. SOLARCYCLE recicla paneles solares para recuperar metales valiosos. Greyparrot usa inteligencia artificial para mejorar la separación de residuos en centros de reciclaje. Otras startups crean etiquetas de papel, recubrimientos a base de algas para empaques y enzimas que descomponen plásticos.

**Impacto** – Estas startups ayudan a reducir residuos y contaminación en sectores como la moda, la electrónica y el empaque. Han firmado acuerdos con grandes marcas como Zara, H&M y L’Oréal, y están construyendo fábricas que pueden procesar grandes cantidades de materiales. Por ejemplo, la planta de Circ en Francia reciclará 70,000 toneladas de textiles al año, y SOLARCYCLE planea una instalación para reciclar millones de paneles solares. Su trabajo evita que estos materiales terminen en vertederos o contaminen el ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – Muchas de estas empresas están pasando de probar sus ideas a producir a gran escala. Nuevas leyes en Europa y EE.UU. también impulsan más reciclaje y hacen responsables a las empresas por sus desechos de empaque. Con más de 670 millones de dólares invertidos en startups de economía circular en 2025, estas innovaciones podrían volverse comunes pronto, cambiando cómo fabricamos y reutilizamos productos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Startups innovadoras están transformando los desechos en recursos valiosos a gran escala, ayudando a las industrias a reciclar más y a reducir daños ambientales en los próximos años.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of interconnected industrial-scale recycling facilities shaped like stylized textile fibers, solar panels, and packaging materials, linked by flowing streams of vibrant, recycled raw materials glowing softly in natural earth tones, set against a calm horizon symbolizing global growth and innovation in circular economy startups.

articles.title: The beautiful Venetian plant with a secret climate superpower
articles.content:
Venice’s landmarks teem with tourists — so many, in fact, that the city has had to implement restrictions, like banning guides from using loudspeakers. But just outside the famous canals and resplendent architecture sits an ecosystem that teems with less obnoxious forms of life: the Venetian lagoon. For millennia, its marshes have hosted a bevy of flora and fauna, and for centuries have protected the city from invasion by its enemies.



Now, protecting this habitat, and others like it, can help protect people and the planet. Traipsing through the wetland and sampling plants, researchers identified a carbon-capturing powerhouse, known as sea lavender, of the genus Limonium. By restoring these biomes, conservationists would not only boost local biodiversity, but also ensure its ability to trap that planet-warming gas. “Salt marshes are not only sites of carbon sequestration,” said Tegan Blount, a geoscientist at Italy’s University of Padova, lead author of a new paper describing the research. “Their conservation also protects many other ecosystem services, which are vitally important from a local to global scale.”



Aboveground, sea lavender is a stunner. True to its name — though technically it isn’t lavender — it produces lovely purple flowers that attract pollinators, thus supporting biodiversity. Unlike terrestrial lavender, though, Limonium tolerates salty, water-logged conditions, allowing it to thrive in the wetlands of the Venice lagoon. “During summer, the salt marsh meadows are tinted purple by an undulating mass of sea lavender flowers, rife with bees and other insects,” Blount said.

Sea lavender’s root system holds a surprising amount of carbon. Photo courtesy of Tegan Blount

While Limonium is great to look at and all, these researchers were more interested in what’s belowground. Instead of a network of fine filaments, sea lavender’s mature rhizome system grows like a hand reaching up from the soil, with foliage sprouting from the fingertips at the surface. (That’s them in the photo.)

This impacts the Venetian marshes in several ways. With its sturdy root system and foliage, sea lavender anchors the waterlogged soil, generates organic material, and traps sediment, which reduces erosion and habitat loss in the face of pressures like sea level rise. It also can create a more stable and amenable environment for other salt-tolerant species, further boosting biodiversity. “So it can also be a stepping stone,” Blount said.



Even after it dies, this marvelous plant’s root system can persist for long periods, continuing to engineer the mud. The study found that compared to other marshy species in the area, like those in the genera Sarcocornia and Juncus, Limonium creates much more biomass below the ground than above it, and markedly enhances the organic carbon content of the sediment. In fact, sea lavender can retain 12 times as much biomass underground as you see growing topside.



By protecting these ecosystems, sea lavender can prosper alongside other species, so conservationists wouldn’t need to constantly tend to them, Blount said. Species of Limonium grow all over the world, too, from the coasts of North America to Africa to Asia. Restoring those habitats, then, would benefit biodiversity while enhancing carbon sequestration and storage. Additionally, healthy wetlands help absorb the force of hurricane storm surges, mitigating the inundation of coastal cities.



Properly restored, coastal ecosystems can be self-sustaining. Infrastructure like sea walls, on the other hand, is expensive to construct and maintain, especially as ocean levels rise. Given enough space to creep inland, wetlands can adapt. “These systems can keep up pace with sea level rise, as long as they can migrate backwards,” said Emily Landis, global climate adaptation and resilience director at The Nature Conservancy, who wasn’t involved in the study. “That means they can still provide that essential adaptation, flood reduction benefit.”



They bring economic benefits too, when conservationists work with Indigenous communities to determine how they use these ecosystems. Subsistence fishing, for instance, can be done in a measured way that ensures piscine populations don’t crash, which would be terrible both for the ecosystem and the humans that rely on it. “They know how to take care of their coastline,” Landis said. “They know what is sustainable.”



In the Venetian lagoon, fishers have long used valli da pesca, essentially ponds that function as artificial ecosystems. This provides shelter for baby fish to grow big enough to harvest. Taking animals out of these habitats may sound counterproductive, but in a way it incentivizes protecting these areas. “So conservation is not just a matter of preserving the environment, but also to have something back,” said Alice Stocco, an ecologist at the Ca’ Foscari University of Venice, who studies the valli da pesca but wasn’t involved in the new paper.



The value of sea lavender, then, isn’t just how much carbon it captures in the Venetian lagoon, but the habitats — and therefore economic and ecological benefits — it provides. “An ecosystem — nature in general — has its own value, which is intrinsic and sometimes cannot be measured,” Stocco said. “Healthy ecosystems allow for healthy people.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the discovery and importance of sea lavender in Venice's wetlands as a powerful carbon sink and biodiversity booster, emphasizing its role in climate change mitigation, ecosystem restoration, and coastal protection. It discusses broad environmental benefits and sustainable economic practices, providing detailed context and significance for global conservation efforts.
articles.personality_title: Venice’s sea lavender: a natural ally against climate change
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Venice is famous for its canals and old buildings, but nearby lies the Venetian lagoon, a wetland full of plants and animals. These wetlands have protected the city for centuries and now are important for fighting climate change.

**What happened** – Scientists studied a plant called sea lavender (Limonium) that grows in Venice’s salt marshes. Unlike regular lavender, sea lavender lives in salty, water-covered soil. Researchers found that its roots store a large amount of carbon underground. This helps trap carbon dioxide, a gas that warms the planet.

**Impact** – Sea lavender’s strong roots hold soil in place, reduce erosion, and create a good home for other plants and insects. It can store 12 times more carbon below ground than what grows above. Protecting this plant and its wetland habitat helps keep the environment healthy and can reduce flooding caused by rising sea levels.

**What’s next step** – Conservation groups want to restore wetlands with sea lavender and similar plants worldwide. Healthy wetlands can adapt to rising seas naturally and support local communities by providing sustainable fishing and protecting coastlines.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Sea lavender in Venice’s wetlands is a powerful natural tool that captures carbon, protects the land, and supports both nature and people.
articles.personality_title_fr: La lavande de mer de Venise : un allié naturel contre le changement climatique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Venise est célèbre pour ses canaux et ses vieux bâtiments, mais à proximité se trouve la lagune vénitienne, une zone humide remplie de plantes et d’animaux. Ces zones humides protègent la ville depuis des siècles et sont maintenant importantes pour lutter contre le changement climatique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont étudié une plante appelée lavande de mer (Limonium) qui pousse dans les marais salés de Venise. Contrairement à la lavande ordinaire, la lavande de mer vit dans un sol salé et inondé. Les chercheurs ont découvert que ses racines stockent une grande quantité de carbone sous terre. Cela aide à piéger le dioxyde de carbone, un gaz qui réchauffe la planète.

**Impact** – Les racines solides de la lavande de mer maintiennent le sol en place, réduisent l’érosion et créent un bon habitat pour d’autres plantes et insectes. Elle peut stocker 12 fois plus de carbone sous terre que ce qui pousse au-dessus. Protéger cette plante et son habitat humide aide à garder l’environnement sain et peut réduire les inondations causées par la montée des eaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les groupes de conservation veulent restaurer les zones humides avec la lavande de mer et des plantes similaires dans le monde entier. Les zones humides saines peuvent s’adapter naturellement à la montée des mers et soutenir les communautés locales grâce à une pêche durable et à la protection des côtes.

**Message clé en une phrase** – La lavande de mer dans les zones humides de Venise est un outil naturel puissant qui capture le carbone, protège la terre et soutient à la fois la nature et les humains.
articles.personality_title_es: La lavanda marina de Venecia: un aliado natural contra el cambio climático
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Venecia es famosa por sus canales y edificios antiguos, pero cerca está la laguna veneciana, un humedal lleno de plantas y animales. Estos humedales han protegido la ciudad durante siglos y ahora son importantes para combatir el cambio climático.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos estudiaron una planta llamada lavanda marina (Limonium) que crece en los pantanos salados de Venecia. A diferencia de la lavanda común, la lavanda marina vive en suelos salados y anegados. Los investigadores descubrieron que sus raíces almacenan una gran cantidad de carbono bajo tierra. Esto ayuda a atrapar el dióxido de carbono, un gas que calienta el planeta.

**Impacto** – Las raíces fuertes de la lavanda marina mantienen el suelo en su lugar, reducen la erosión y crean buen hogar para otras plantas e insectos. Puede almacenar 12 veces más carbono bajo tierra que lo que crece arriba. Proteger esta planta y su hábitat ayuda a mantener el medio ambiente sano y puede reducir las inundaciones causadas por la subida del nivel del mar.

**Próximo paso** – Los grupos de conservación quieren restaurar humedales con lavanda marina y plantas similares en todo el mundo. Los humedales saludables pueden adaptarse naturalmente a la subida del mar y apoyar a las comunidades locales mediante la pesca sostenible y la protección de las costas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La lavanda marina en los humedales de Venecia es una herramienta natural poderosa que captura carbono, protege la tierra y apoya tanto a la naturaleza como a las personas.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, detailed painting of a vibrant Venetian salt marsh at sunset, featuring clusters of delicate purple sea lavender flowers with their distinctive hand-like root systems visible beneath the translucent waterlogged soil, surrounded by calm lagoon waters and subtle silhouettes of fish swimming in protected ponds, all rendered in soft natural tones to evoke harmony between nature’s beauty and its powerful role in climate resilience.

articles.title: Green hydrogen has a hidden problem and scientists may have fixed it
articles.summary: Green hydrogen could be a game-changer for the clean energy transition—but right now, it’s too expensive and still relies on harmful “forever chemicals.” A new EU-backed project called SUPREME aims to fix that by reinventing how hydrogen is made. Led by the University of Southern Denmark with partners across Europe, researchers are developing a PFAS-free electrolysis system that slashes the use of rare metals like iridium and dramatically cuts costs.
articles.content:
Green hydrogen is widely seen as a key pillar of the global shift away from fossil fuels. Yet making it at scale remains both costly and environmentally complicated. One of the leading production methods, PEM (proton exchange membrane) electrolysis, works especially well when electricity from wind and solar power rises and falls. However, it is still far more expensive than producing hydrogen from fossil fuels.

There are also environmental concerns. Current PEM systems depend on so called forever chemicals (PFAS), substances the European Union plans to phase out because of their environmental and health risks. Addressing both the high costs and the chemical concerns is the goal of the EU funded SUPREME project. Over the next three years, researchers led by the University of Southern Denmark, working with Graz University of Technology (TU Graz) and other partners, will develop a PFAS-free electrolysis system that is more efficient and uses far smaller amounts of critical raw materials such as iridium. The aim is to make green hydrogen significantly more affordable and sustainable.

Making Green Hydrogen Cleaner and Cheaper

"Hydrogen is used as a raw material in very large quantities, and this will continue to increase in the future. These include the production of ammonia, methanol production and the steel industry," says Merit Bodner from the Institute of Chemical Engineering and Environmental Technology at TU Graz.

"If we succeed in avoiding the use of harmful substances in the production of green hydrogen and we can also bring it to a similar price level as fossil hydrogen in economic terms, we will have taken an important step towards the green transition. This also makes it more attractive for other applications, such as storing surplus energy from renewables."

Hydrogen already plays a central role in major industrial processes, and demand is expected to grow. Making its production cleaner and more competitive with fossil based hydrogen could accelerate its use not only in heavy industry, but also as a way to store excess renewable energy.

PFAS Free Materials and Advanced Membranes

TU Graz has a leading role in evaluating safer alternatives. Bodner's team is reviewing commercially available PFAS-free materials and comparing their performance with current industry standards. A key question is whether these more sustainable materials can match the durability and efficiency required for continuous industrial use.

Meanwhile, the Turkish Science and Technology Council TÜBITAK is focusing on membrane development. The group is working on a new generation of microporous PFAS-free membranes designed for use in future electrolysis systems.

Cutting and Recycling Iridium

Another major focus is reducing reliance on iridium, a costly platinum group metal used in PEM electrolysis. The University of Southern Denmark and the British metal and catalyst company Ceimig are exploring ways to cut iridium use by up to 75 per cent. They are also developing recycling methods that could recover around 90 per cent of the iridium still needed.

Additional partners are contributing specialized components. Fraunhofer ISE in Germany is manufacturing the membrane electrode units, while the Norwegian hydrogen company Element One Energy AS (EoneE) is designing a new rotating electrolyser intended to improve system performance.

The project is funded through CETPartnership, the Clean Energy Transition Partnership under the 2024 joint call for research proposals, and is co funded by the European Commission (GA N°101069750).
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant EU-backed scientific project developing a PFAS-free, more affordable green hydrogen production method that reduces reliance on rare metals and harmful chemicals, addressing both environmental and economic challenges. This advancement has broad implications for clean energy transition and industrial sustainability, with detailed context and collaborative efforts described.
articles.personality_title: New EU project aims to make green hydrogen cleaner and cheaper
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Green hydrogen is an important clean energy source that can help reduce the use of fossil fuels. It is made using a process called proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolysis, which uses electricity from wind or solar power. However, this method is expensive and relies on harmful chemicals called PFAS that the European Union wants to stop using.

**What happened** – A new EU-funded project called SUPREME, led by the University of Southern Denmark and partners across Europe, is working to improve how green hydrogen is produced. They are developing a new PEM electrolysis system that does not use PFAS chemicals and uses much less iridium, a rare and costly metal. The project also includes efforts to recycle most of the iridium used.

**Impact** – This project could make green hydrogen cheaper and safer for the environment by removing harmful chemicals and reducing expensive materials. Since hydrogen is widely used in industries like steel, ammonia, and methanol production, making it cleaner and more affordable could speed up the shift to renewable energy and help store surplus power from wind and solar sources.

**What's next step** – Over the next three years, the research teams will test and improve new materials and membranes that do not contain PFAS. They will also develop better recycling methods for iridium and design new equipment to make the electrolysis process more efficient. If successful, this could lead to large-scale production of cleaner green hydrogen across Europe.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The SUPREME project is developing a safer, cheaper way to produce green hydrogen by removing harmful chemicals and cutting the use of rare metals, supporting cleaner energy for the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau projet européen vise à rendre l’hydrogène vert plus propre et moins cher
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’hydrogène vert est une source d’énergie propre importante qui peut aider à réduire l’utilisation des combustibles fossiles. Il est produit grâce à un procédé appelé électrolyse à membrane échangeuse de protons (PEM), qui utilise l’électricité provenant de l’éolien ou du solaire. Cependant, cette méthode est coûteuse et utilise des produits chimiques nocifs appelés PFAS que l’Union européenne souhaite éliminer.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un nouveau projet financé par l’UE, nommé SUPREME, dirigé par l’Université du Sud du Danemark en collaboration avec des partenaires européens, travaille à améliorer la production d’hydrogène vert. Ils développent un nouveau système d’électrolyse PEM sans PFAS et utilisant beaucoup moins d’iridium, un métal rare et cher. Le projet comprend aussi des méthodes pour recycler la plupart de l’iridium utilisé.

**Impact** – Ce projet pourrait rendre l’hydrogène vert moins cher et plus sûr pour l’environnement en supprimant les produits chimiques dangereux et en réduisant les matériaux coûteux. Comme l’hydrogène est largement utilisé dans des industries comme l’acier, l’ammoniac et le méthanol, le rendre plus propre et abordable pourrait accélérer la transition vers les énergies renouvelables et aider à stocker l’énergie excédentaire du vent et du soleil.

**Prochaine étape** – Au cours des trois prochaines années, les équipes de recherche testeront et amélioreront de nouveaux matériaux et membranes sans PFAS. Elles développeront aussi de meilleures méthodes de recyclage de l’iridium et concevront de nouveaux équipements pour rendre l’électrolyse plus efficace. En cas de succès, cela pourrait conduire à une production à grande échelle d’hydrogène vert plus propre en Europe.

**Phrase clé** – Le projet SUPREME développe une méthode plus sûre et moins chère pour produire de l’hydrogène vert en supprimant les produits chimiques nocifs et en réduisant l’utilisation de métaux rares, soutenant une énergie plus propre pour l’avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo proyecto europeo busca hacer el hidrógeno verde más limpio y barato
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El hidrógeno verde es una fuente de energía limpia importante que puede ayudar a reducir el uso de combustibles fósiles. Se produce mediante un proceso llamado electrólisis de membrana de intercambio protónico (PEM), que usa electricidad de la energía eólica o solar. Sin embargo, este método es caro y depende de químicos dañinos llamados PFAS, que la Unión Europea quiere eliminar.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo proyecto financiado por la UE llamado SUPREME, liderado por la Universidad del Sur de Dinamarca junto con socios europeos, está trabajando para mejorar la producción de hidrógeno verde. Están desarrollando un nuevo sistema de electrólisis PEM sin químicos PFAS y que usa mucho menos iridio, un metal raro y costoso. El proyecto también incluye métodos para reciclar la mayoría del iridio usado.

**Impacto** – Este proyecto podría hacer que el hidrógeno verde sea más barato y seguro para el medio ambiente al eliminar químicos dañinos y reducir materiales costosos. Como el hidrógeno se usa mucho en industrias como la del acero, amoníaco y metanol, hacerlo más limpio y accesible podría acelerar la transición a energías renovables y ayudar a almacenar energía sobrante del viento y el sol.

**Próximo paso** – Durante los próximos tres años, los equipos de investigación probarán y mejorarán nuevos materiales y membranas sin PFAS. También desarrollarán mejores métodos para reciclar iridio y diseñarán nuevos equipos para hacer la electrólisis más eficiente. Si tienen éxito, esto podría llevar a una producción en gran escala de hidrógeno verde más limpio en Europa.

**Frase clave** – El proyecto SUPREME está desarrollando una forma más segura y barata de producir hidrógeno verde al eliminar químicos dañinos y reducir el uso de metales raros, apoyando una energía más limpia para el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a stylized green hydrogen molecule glowing gently at the center, surrounded by abstract representations of clean energy sources like softly swirling wind turbines and solar panels made of simple geometric shapes, with delicate, translucent membranes and shimmering metallic elements symbolizing advanced PFAS-free materials and recycled iridium gently encircling the molecule, all set against a calm, natural background of soft earth and sky tones conveying sustainability and innovation.

articles.title: Ireland’s Old Irish Goat has survived 3,000 years
articles.summary: The Old Irish Goat isn’t just part of folklore — it’s genetically linked to goats that lived in Ireland 3,000 years ago. Scientists analyzed ancient remains and discovered that today’s rare breed shares its strongest DNA ties with Late Bronze Age animals. The finding suggests an unbroken Irish lineage stretching back millennia. It also adds urgency to protecting this critically endangered survivor of Ireland’s agricultural past.
articles.content:
A new scientific study has found that the Old Irish Goat shares a genetic connection with goats that lived in Ireland about 3,000 years ago during the Late Bronze Age. The findings indicate that this rare native breed represents an unbroken Irish lineage that stretches back thousands of years.

The research was led by University College Dublin in partnership with Queen's University Belfast and international collaborators. Published in the Journal of Archaeological Science, the biomolecular and archaeological investigation offers a fresh perspective on Ireland's farming history and strengthens the case for conserving the Old Irish Goat as a living connection to early agricultural communities.

Oldest Goat Remains Found in Ireland

Scientists examined goat bones recovered from Haughey's Fort in Co Armagh, a hillfort dating to roughly 1100-900 BCE, as well as remains from the medieval town of Carrickfergus in Co Antrim.

Using radiocarbon dating along with genetic and protein testing, the team confirmed that the Late Bronze Age specimens are the oldest goat remains yet identified in Ireland.

When researchers compared the genomes of these ancient animals with modern breeds, they found the closest genetic match was the critically endangered Old Irish Goat still surviving today.

"Combining genetics, proteomics, and archaeological science has allowed us a glimpse of our animals hundreds and thousands of years ago -- and how their descendants likely still live with us, as part of our biocultural heritage," said co-lead author Assistant Professor Kevin Daly, School of Agriculture and Food Science.

Folklore, Farming, and the Wild Irish Goat

Historically known as 'an Gabhar Fiáin' -- the wild goat -- the Old Irish Goat occupies a unique place in Irish folklore. Today it persists in small wild-roaming (feral) herds. For generations, it has symbolized resilience and rural life, valued for its toughness, ability to graze on marginal land, and nutrient-dense milk that supported small farmers.

While cattle dominate Ireland's mythological traditions, goats tend to appear in local customs, place names, and seasonal practices.

One of the best-known cultural links is Puck Fair in Killorglin, Co Kerry, considered one of Ireland's oldest festivals.

Each August, tradition holds that a goat was captured from the mountains and crowned "King Puck," presiding over three days of festivities. Although historians still debate how the celebration began, the festival reflects the goat's deep ties to Ireland's landscape and community identity.

"Despite thousands of years, changing farming practices, and recent decline, these goats have retained a remarkable genetic connection to their ancestors, and to the island's agricultural past," said Dr. Jolijn Erven, co-lead author of the study.

Professor Eileen Murphy, co-lead author from Queen's University Belfast, explained that goats are often underrepresented in archaeological research because their bones are difficult to distinguish from sheep remains. "Goats tend to get overlooked compared to sheep in the archaeological record because it is notoriously difficult to distinguish between their bones. There is an assumption that sheep would have been more important than goats in the past but historical sources suggest that herds of goats may have been kept to supply a trade in skins from ports such as Carrickfergus," she said.

Protein Fingerprinting and Genetic Analysis

To make certain they were studying goat remains, researchers first used protein fingerprinting (ZooMS), which identifies species by analyzing tiny traces of preserved collagen.

They then extracted and sequenced ancient DNA, comparing the genetic material from Bronze Age and medieval goats with hundreds of modern breeds from around the world.

The results showed that both prehistoric and medieval Irish goats are most closely related to today's Old Irish Goat. This points to a striking continuity of goat populations on the island for more than three millennia.

Modern Genetic Bottleneck Raises Concerns

The research also highlights more recent changes in the breed's genetic diversity.

Medieval goats displayed a broader range of genetic variation, while modern Old Irish Goats show clear signs of inbreeding. Scientists link this pattern to a sharp population decline in recent decades.

This suggests that the current genetic bottleneck is a recent development rather than a long-standing feature of Irish goat populations.

"This research is a huge milestone for the Old Irish Goat, and provides powerful scientific validation of what local communities and conservationists have long believed -- that the Old Irish Goat represents a living piece of our ancient heritage. It also underscores the urgency of protecting this critically endangered breed, which carries within it a living genetic record of Ireland's ancient past," said Sinead Keane, from The Old Irish Goat Society.

"The advancements in biomolecular and genetic analysis are very exciting, and have now opened up a new opportunity to revisit Ireland's archaeological and archaeogenetic records, which may in time reveal that Ireland's early goat history is even richer than currently understood."

Co-lead author Dr. Judith Findlater from Queen's University Belfast passed away before the study was published. Portions of the research were conducted as part of her PhD work on Medieval Carrickfergus.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery linking the critically endangered Old Irish Goat to a 3,000-year-old lineage, highlighting its importance as a living genetic and cultural heritage. The study has broad implications for conservation, biodiversity, and understanding of Ireland's agricultural history, emphasizing the urgent need to protect this rare breed. The story is focused, detailed, and presents a meaningful positive impact on environmental conservation and cultural preservation.
articles.personality_title: Ireland’s Old Irish Goat traced back 3,000 years through DNA
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied ancient animal remains to understand Ireland’s farming history. The Old Irish Goat, a rare breed still found in Ireland today, was thought to have deep roots but its exact origins were unclear.

**What happened** – Researchers from University College Dublin, Queen’s University Belfast, and other partners examined goat bones from Ireland’s Late Bronze Age (about 3,000 years ago) and medieval times. Using radiocarbon dating, protein fingerprinting, and DNA analysis, they found that today’s Old Irish Goat is directly related to those ancient goats. This confirms the breed has an unbroken history in Ireland stretching thousands of years.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it proves the Old Irish Goat is a living link to Ireland’s ancient farming past. The breed holds unique genetic information that has survived many changes over time. However, scientists also found that modern Old Irish Goats have less genetic diversity due to recent population decline. This highlights the urgent need to protect them before their ancient lineage is lost.

**What’s next step** – Conservation groups and scientists will likely use this new knowledge to help save the Old Irish Goat. The research also opens doors to studying other ancient Irish animals with modern genetic tools. Protecting this breed can preserve both biodiversity and Ireland’s cultural heritage.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Modern Old Irish Goats carry the genetic legacy of their ancestors from 3,000 years ago, making their protection crucial for preserving Ireland’s natural and cultural history.
articles.personality_title_fr: La chèvre irlandaise ancienne reliée génétiquement à il y a 3 000 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps les restes d’animaux anciens pour mieux comprendre l’histoire agricole de l’Irlande. La chèvre irlandaise ancienne, une race rare encore présente aujourd’hui, avait des origines incertaines.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université College Dublin, de l’Université Queen’s de Belfast et d’autres partenaires ont examiné des os de chèvre datant de l’âge du bronze tardif (il y a environ 3 000 ans) et du Moyen Âge. Grâce à la datation au radiocarbone, à l’analyse des protéines et à l’ADN, ils ont découvert que la chèvre irlandaise ancienne d’aujourd’hui est directement liée à ces chèvres anciennes, prouvant une continuité génétique.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que cette chèvre est un lien vivant avec le passé agricole ancien de l’Irlande. La race conserve des informations génétiques uniques malgré les changements au fil du temps. Mais les scientifiques ont aussi constaté une baisse de la diversité génétique récente, ce qui rend leur protection urgente.

**Prochaine étape** – Les groupes de conservation et les chercheurs vont probablement utiliser ces résultats pour aider à sauver la chèvre irlandaise ancienne. Cette étude ouvre aussi la voie à l’exploration d’autres animaux anciens d’Irlande avec des outils génétiques modernes. Protéger cette race, c’est préserver la biodiversité et le patrimoine culturel.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les chèvres irlandaises anciennes d’aujourd’hui portent l’héritage génétique de leurs ancêtres d’il y a 3 000 ans, rendant leur protection essentielle pour l’histoire naturelle et culturelle de l’Irlande.
articles.personality_title_es: La cabra irlandesa antigua vinculada genéticamente hace 3.000 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado restos antiguos de animales para entender la historia agrícola de Irlanda. La cabra irlandesa antigua, una raza rara que aún existe hoy, tenía orígenes poco claros.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de University College Dublin, Queen’s University Belfast y otros colaboradores analizaron huesos de cabra de la Edad del Bronce tardío (hace unos 3.000 años) y de la época medieval. Usando datación por radiocarbono, análisis de proteínas y ADN, encontraron que la cabra irlandesa antigua actual está directamente relacionada con esas cabras antiguas, confirmando una línea genética continua.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque demuestra que esta cabra es un vínculo vivo con el pasado agrícola antiguo de Irlanda. La raza conserva información genética única a pesar de los cambios a lo largo del tiempo. Sin embargo, los científicos también notaron una menor diversidad genética reciente, lo que hace urgente su protección.

**Próximo paso** – Los grupos de conservación y científicos probablemente usarán esta información para ayudar a salvar la cabra irlandesa antigua. La investigación también abre la posibilidad de estudiar otros animales antiguos irlandeses con herramientas genéticas modernas. Proteger esta raza ayuda a conservar la biodiversidad y el patrimonio cultural.

**Una frase que resume** – Las cabras irlandesas antiguas actuales llevan el legado genético de sus antepasados de hace 3.000 años, haciendo crucial su protección para la historia natural y cultural de Irlanda.
articles.image_prompt: An intricately detailed warm-toned painting of a sturdy Old Irish Goat standing atop a gentle green hill beside ancient, weathered goat bones partially buried in the earth, with faint, ethereal outlines of a Late Bronze Age hillfort and a medieval town softly blending into the background under a calm sky.
